unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Year 4 English! In this unit you will explore daily life and communication — the things people do every day and the ways we share ideas with one another. You will read about routines, letters and mail, working hard, and being fair. You will learn how people communicate in different languages, and why steady effort helps us gain new skills. By the end, you will be able to interview someone about their daily work, write clearly about your own routine, and use new grammar to make your sentences stronger — just like Sophia does in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a small group of words each day.
Work through the mini-lessons in the Grammar file, and check your answers.
Read the Story, “Fair Effort, Daily Gains”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see how much you have learned.

visual.alt: A learner organising a daily routine and practising clear communication

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Letters and packages that are sent or delivered.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I received a letter in the mail today.
practiceSentences: I received a letter in the mail today.
Musa checked the mail before breakfast every single morning.
Did any mail arrive for Grandmother this week?
"The mail is late because of the rain," said Father.
I love sorting the mail when the postman visits our house.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - i - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'mail', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A way people talk and write to share ideas and feelings.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: English is a language that many people speak around the world.
practiceSentences: English is a language that many people speak around the world.
Samira speaks one language at home and another at school.
Which language do you use when you talk to Grandfather?
"Arabic is a beautiful language," said our teacher proudly.
Learning a new language takes patience, but I enjoy it.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - a - n - g - u - a - g - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'language', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who belongs to a country and has rights there.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Every citizen has rights and rules to follow.
practiceSentences: Every citizen has rights and rules to follow.
A good citizen helps neighbours and keeps the streets clean.
What can a young citizen do to help the town?
"Every citizen should vote when they grow up," said Uncle.
I want to be a helpful citizen in my community.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - i - t - i - z - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'citizen', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To have the same opinion, or to say yes to something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I agree with you that reading is fun.
practiceSentences: I agree with you that reading is fun.
Musa and Yusuf agree that football is the best game.
Do you agree that we should share the crayons fairly?
"I agree with your plan," said Aisha with a smile.
My sister and I rarely agree about which book to read.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - g - r - e - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'agree', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To keep doing something without stopping.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Please continue reading your book after lunch.
practiceSentences: Please continue reading your book after lunch.
The rain stopped, so the children could continue their game.
Will you continue your story after dinner tonight?
"Please continue writing until the bell rings," said Mrs Ali.
I will continue practising my spelling words every evening.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - n - t - i - n - u - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'continue', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To get something you want, or to become better at something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I want to gain new skills by practising every day.
practiceSentences: I want to gain new skills by practising every day.
Ishmael worked hard to gain a place on the team.
What do you gain from waking up early each day?
"You gain strength by eating healthy food," said Mother.
I hope to gain more confidence when I read aloud.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - a - i - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'gain', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The hard work you put into doing something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It took a lot of effort to finish my homework on time.
practiceSentences: It took a lot of effort to finish my homework on time.
Samira put great effort into her poster about kindness.
How much effort did it take to build that model?
"Your effort makes me proud," said Father after the race.
With a little more effort, I finished the whole puzzle.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - f - f - o - r - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'effort', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To learn something very well.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She mastered the dance after many tries.
practiceSentences: She mastered the dance after many tries.
Aisha wants to master the new times tables this term.
How long did it take you to master swimming?
"Practise daily and you will master it," said the coach.
I finally mastered tying my shoelaces without any help.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - s - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'master', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To keep something in good condition, or to make it stay the same.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I help my father maintain the garden by watering the plants.
practiceSentences: I help my father maintain the garden by watering the plants.
Yusuf helps maintain the school garden by pulling weeds.
How do you maintain your bicycle in the winter?
"We must maintain a tidy classroom," said our teacher.
I maintain my reading habit by visiting the library weekly.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - i - n - t - a - i - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'maintain', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How fast something moves or happens.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The car moved at a high speed.
practiceSentences: The car moved at a high speed.
The train passed our village at a very high speed.
What speed can a cheetah reach when it runs?
"Slow your speed on the wet path," warned Father.
I love the speed of my new bicycle down the hill.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - p - e - e - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'speed', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having an equal, steady amount on each side; not too much of one thing.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I keep a good balance between school and free time.
practiceSentences: I keep a good balance between school and free time.
Musa keeps a healthy balance between homework and play.
How do you balance schoolwork with helping at home?
"Good balance helps you ride a bike," said my brother.
I try to balance my time between reading and football.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - a - l - a - n - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'balance', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Happening every day.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We have a daily reading time in class.
practiceSentences: We have a daily reading time in class.
Samira waters the plants as part of her daily jobs.
What is your favourite part of the daily timetable?
"Our daily walk keeps us healthy," said Grandmother.
I write in my journal daily before I go to sleep.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - a - i - l - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'daily', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something that happens most of the time, or is normal.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We had our usual lunch at noon.
practiceSentences: We had our usual lunch at noon.
Yusuf sat in his usual seat near the window.
Shall we meet at the usual place after school?
"Take your usual route home," Mother reminded me.
My usual breakfast is porridge with honey and warm milk.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: u - s - u - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'usual', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Treating everyone equally and in the same way.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It is fair to let everyone have a turn.
practiceSentences: It is fair to let everyone have a turn.
The teacher made a fair rule so everyone got a turn.
Is it fair that Aisha always goes first?
"A fair game means nobody cheats," said the referee.
I try to be fair when I share sweets with my brother.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - a - i - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'fair', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something that is needed or very important.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It is necessary to wear a helmet when riding a bike.
practiceSentences: It is necessary to wear a helmet when riding a bike.
Warm coats are necessary when the weather turns cold.
Is it necessary to bring our books on the trip?
"Sleep is necessary for a healthy body," said the nurse.
I packed everything necessary for our visit to Grandmother.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - e - c - e - s - s - a - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'necessary', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To say that something will not happen anymore.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We had to cancel the picnic because of the rain.
practiceSentences: We had to cancel the picnic because of the rain.
The heavy rain forced the school to cancel sports day.
Why did they cancel the trip to the museum?
"We may cancel practice if the field floods," said the coach.
I felt sad when we had to cancel my party.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - a - n - c - e - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'cancel', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To go away from a place.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will leave school at three o'clock.
practiceSentences: We will leave school at three o'clock.
The birds leave the pond when winter arrives.
What time do you leave for school each morning?
"Please leave your shoes by the door," said Grandmother.
I never leave my homework until the last minute.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - e - a - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'leave', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A time when there is calm and quiet, with no fighting or trouble.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The garden was full of peace and quiet.
practiceSentences: The garden was full of peace and quiet.
After the noisy market, the quiet mosque felt full of peace.
Do you feel peace when you read by the window?
"Let us work in peace," whispered the librarian kindly.
I enjoy the peace of early morning before everyone wakes.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - e - a - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'peace', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Used to show that something is very true or strong.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I really like our reading time.
practiceSentences: I really like our reading time.
Aisha really enjoyed the story about the clever tortoise.
Do you really think our team can win tomorrow?
"That cake was really delicious," said Uncle Yusuf.
I really want to visit the sea during the holidays.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - a - l - l - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'really', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: In a way that is easy to see, hear, or understand.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She spoke clearly so everyone could hear her.
practiceSentences: She spoke clearly so everyone could hear her.
Musa wrote his name clearly at the top of the page.
Can you see the mountains clearly from your window?
"Speak clearly so the whole class can hear," said Mrs Ali.
I remember that rainy Eid morning very clearly.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - l - e - a - r - l - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'clearly', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk and write about your daily routine and the things you do every day.
Explain why communication — letters, mail, and language — matters to a community.
Use verb patterns correctly (for example, “She agrees to send the mail”).
Add adverbs to the present simple to say how and how often you do things.
Use have got and has got to talk about what people own or have.
Ask clear Wh- questions and interview questions to find out information.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Give instructions using imperatives, and join ideas with conjunctions.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Interview a person about their work and write a fair, clear report about them.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: A Day at the Posthouse
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Mr. Mzee works at the posthouse in his village. Every day he sorts the mail, helps people send letters, and reads letters aloud for those who cannot read well. “Not everyone has a phone,” he says. “A letter can bring peace and make someone feel remembered.” Mr. Mzee speaks two languages, so he can help almost everyone in town. His work is not fast, but he does it with effort and care. “Some days are busy, some are quiet,” he says, “but every day matters.”

readings.title: My Daily Routine
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Every morning I help my mother sweep the floor and boil the water. Then I walk to school with my friends. I usually arrive early. In class we have a daily reading time, which I really like. After school I do my chores and my homework. I try to keep a good balance between my work and my free time. If I use my time well, I can do it all.

readings.title: An Interview (script)
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Sophia:  “May I interview you for my school report?”
Mr. Mzee:  “Of course. That sounds like a good project.”
Sophia:  “What do you do in your daily work?”
Mr. Mzee:  “I sort mail, help people send letters, and talk to visitors.”
Sophia:  “Why is mail still important?”
Mr. Mzee:  “Because not everyone has a phone. A letter can make someone feel remembered.”

readings.title: Fair Effort, Daily Gains
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Every morning, Sophia helped her mother sweep the floor and boil the water before school. She liked to stay busy, because she believed that small tasks showed big effort. She also tried to work at speed, so that she still had time to read.
At school, the head teacher pinned up a notice. “Writing Contest!” it said. “Write a report about someone in your community. The winner gets a dictionary!”
Sophia smiled. She wanted to improve her language and master new words, and a dictionary would help her learn faster. But she still had homework, chores, and her daily reading. “I must find a balance,” she told herself. “If I use my time well, I can do it all.”
That evening, her mother asked, “Can you manage this contest and your schoolwork?”
“I think so,” said Sophia. “I will try my best and continue learning.”
She thought about who to write about. There were many important people in her village, but one stood out — Mr. Mzee, the kind citizen who worked at the posthouse. He sorted and delivered the mail every day.
After class the next day, Sophia went to visit him. “Mr. Mzee,” she asked, “may I interview you for my school report?”
“Of course,” he smiled. “That sounds like a good project.”
“What do you do in your daily work?” she asked.
“I sort mail, help people send letters, and talk to visitors. I also read letters for people who cannot read well.”
“Why is mail still important?” asked Sophia.
“Because not everyone has a phone. A letter can bring peace, and it can make someone feel remembered.”
Sophia asked if he spoke more than one language. “Yes,” he said. “I had to master English and Kiswahili. Some people speak local languages too. It is necessary, so that I can help everyone.”
“And do you do this job every day?” Sophia asked.
“Yes,” he said. “It is my usual work. Some days are busy and some are quiet, but every day matters.”
Back home, Sophia began her report. She wanted it to be fair and honest, so she added all the details he had told her. She checked her spelling and used her neatest handwriting.
Her father saw her writing. “Take your time,” he said gently. “Do not leave out anything important.”
“I won't,” she replied. “This report has a purpose.”
The next morning it rained hard. A student shouted, “The contest is cancelled!” But the teacher said, “Don't worry! We will hold it in the library. Bring your reports!”
The students sat in a circle and took turns reading. Some were nervous. Others forgot their lines. Sophia waited quietly. When it was her turn, she stood up and read in a clear voice:
“Mr. Mzee is a helpful citizen who delivers the mail and helps others read. He speaks two languages and works with care. He told me that daily effort helps us gain trust and knowledge.”
After she finished, one judge asked, “What did you learn from this?”
“I learned to listen, to write clearly, and to keep going even when it rains,” Sophia said with a smile.
Later that day, the teacher made the announcement. “The winner is Sophia! Her report was honest, clear, and full of meaning.” The whole class clapped, and Sophia stood proudly, holding her prize — a brand-new dictionary.
At home, her mother hugged her. “You did it!”
“Yes,” said Sophia. “I did not stop. I worked hard, and I found a good balance.” She opened her new dictionary and pointed to a word. “Look — ‘Effort: work done with care.’ Now I know the meaning in my heart.”

grammar.title: Verb Patterns
grammar.explanation: A verb pattern is the way we join a verb to the word that comes after it. Some verbs are followed by to + another verb, and some are followed by a verb ending in -ing. Getting the pattern right makes your sentences clear.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Some verbs take to + verb: agree, want, decide, plan, need. → “She agrees to send the mail.”
Some verbs take verb + -ing: enjoy, love, like, finish. → “They enjoy playing football.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She agrees help her friend.”  ✗
Fix: The verb “agree” needs to: “She agrees to help her friend.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of the main verb as a key that opens the door to its partner. Learn which verbs carry the to key and which carry the -ing key.
grammar.practice: Choose the correct form in brackets. | She decided ______ (to go / going) to the library. | They enjoy ______ (to play / playing) basketball. | I want ______ (to learn / learning) a new language. | He finished ______ (to write / writing) his report.

grammar.title: Present Simple + Adverbs
grammar.explanation: We use the present simple to talk about things we do regularly. Adverbs of frequency (how often) and manner (how) make these sentences richer. Instead of “I check the mail,” we can say “I daily check the mail” or “I check the mail clearly.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
For he, she, it, add -s to the verb: “He checks the mail.”
Adverbs of frequency (usually, sometimes, often, rarely, never) usually go before the main verb: “I usually check the mail.”
With the verb to be, the adverb goes after: “She is always happy.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “He check the mail.”  ✗    Fix: “He checks the mail.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Adverbs of frequency are “time helpers” — they usually sit just before the action verb.
grammar.practice: Fill each blank with an adverb: daily, usually, sometimes, rarely. | I ______ go to the park at the weekend, most weeks. | She ______ reads a book before bed, but not always. | They ______ play games after school, only once in a while. | He eats breakfast every single day, so he ______ eats breakfast.

grammar.title: Have Got and Has Got
grammar.explanation: We use have got and has got to show that someone owns or has something. It is a friendly way to talk about what people have.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use have got with I, you, we, they: “We have got a fair system.”
Use has got with he, she, it: “She has got the skills she needs.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “He have got a dog.”  ✗    Fix: “He has got a dog.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: have is for teams (I, you, we, they). has is for a single player (he, she, it).
grammar.practice: Write have got or has got. | I ______ a new book. | She ______ a beautiful garden. | They ______ a great idea. | He ______ a friendly dog.

grammar.title: Wh- Questions
grammar.explanation: Wh- questions start with who, what, where, when, why, or how. They help us ask for exact information, so we can learn about the world and about other people.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Wh- word + helping verb + subject + main verb: “What do you master?”
Match the helping verb to the subject: use does for he/she/it. “What does she like?”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “What do she like?”  ✗    Fix: “What does she like?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Question Word First! Pick the right helper: what for things, who for people, where for places, when for time, why for reasons, how for the way.
grammar.practice: Fill in the correct Wh- word. | ______ is your favourite colour? | ______ do you like to do after school? | ______ does the sun set — in the morning or the evening? | ______ is your best friend?

grammar.title: Imperatives
grammar.explanation: Imperatives are command sentences. We use them to give instructions, directions, or requests, like “Close the door” or “Maintain your effort!” They help us tell someone clearly what to do.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the base verb and leave out “you”: “Close the window.”
For a negative command, start with Don't: “Don't run in the hallway.”
Add please to be polite: “Please wash your hands.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “Going to your seat!”  ✗    Fix: Use the base verb: “Go to your seat.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Imperatives are “commanding verbs” — the verb jumps to the very front of the sentence.
grammar.practice: Write the correct imperative form of the verb in brackets. | ______ (close) the window, please. | ______ (not run) in the hallway. | ______ (finish) your homework before playing. | ______ (help) your brother with his project.

grammar.title: Past Simple (Review) + Conjunctions + Interview Questions
grammar.explanation: We use the past simple to talk about things that already happened. It lets us tell stories and describe what we did yesterday or last week.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Regular verbs: add -ed. “play” → “played”; “walk” → “walked”.
Irregular verbs: learn them by heart. “go” → “went”; “leave” → “left”; “see” → “saw”; “eat” → “ate”.
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “I goed to the store.”  ✗    Fix: “I went to the store.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: For regular verbs: “Add -ed, and you're set!” For irregular verbs, make a list and practise them often.
grammar.practice: Write the past simple of the verb in brackets. | Yesterday, I ______ (play) outside. | They ______ (gain) speed quickly during the race. | She ______ (leave) her bike at the park. | He ______ (see) a rainbow after the rain. | Lesson 7:  Conjunctions | What it means and why it matters | Conjunctions are joining words. They connect ideas so our sentences flow. Instead of “I write mail. I maintain records,” we can say “I write mail and maintain records.” | How to form it | and — to add: “I read and write.” | but — to show a difference: “I like pizza, but I don't like broccoli.” | or — to show a choice: “Would you like cake or ice cream?” | because — to give a reason: “She runs fast because she has a lot of speed.” | A common mistake | Mistake: “I wanted to go outside, and it was raining.”  ✗    Fix: These ideas are opposite, so use but: “I wanted to go outside, but it was raining.”  ✓ | Memory tip | Conjunctions are like glue. Remember: “and adds, but contrasts!” | Practice 7 | Fill in with and, but, or, or because. | I wanted to go to the park, ______ it started to rain. | Would you like to play football ______ basketball? | She studies hard ______ she wants to get good grades. | I like to read books, ______ I also enjoy stories on the radio. | Lesson 8:  Interview Questions | What it means and why it matters | Interview questions are questions we ask to learn about a person, their work, and their ideas — just like Sophia interviews Mr. Mzee in the story. Good questions help us understand others. | How to form it | Start with a question word (What, When, Where, Who, Why, How). | Add a helping verb (do, does, is, can), then the subject, then more information: “How do you gain peace?” | Sometimes no helping verb is needed if the Wh- word is the subject: “Who helps you at work?” | A common mistake | Mistake: “When you leave?”  ✗    Fix: Add the helping verb: “When do you leave?”  ✓ | Memory tip | Question Word First, then the helping verb. Keep the order steady and your questions stay clear. | Practice 8 | Add the correct question word to complete each interview question. | ______ do you like to eat? | ______ is your favourite subject? | ______ do you usually leave for school? | ______ can you ride a bike?

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Describe Your Daily Routine
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to talk for one minute about your day. Use these sentence starters:
“Every morning I ______.”
“I usually ______ before school.”
“After school I ______, and then I ______.”
“I try to keep a balance between ______ and ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use at least two adverbs (daily, usually, really, clearly)? Did you speak clearly enough to understand?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise describe your daily routine and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Interview a Family Member
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Interview someone at home about their daily work, the way Sophia interviewed Mr. Mzee. Ask:
“What do you do in your daily work?”
“Why is your work important?”
“What languages do you speak?”
“What helps you keep going when the work is hard?”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise interview a family member and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Give the Instructions
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family, using imperatives (command sentences):
Think of a simple task, like making tea or tidying a room.
Give the steps as commands: “First, boil the water. Next, add the tea. Then, wait two minutes.”
The other person listens and repeats the steps back to you.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise give the instructions and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: An Interview Report
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Interview a person about their daily work (from Speaking 2). Then write a short, fair report about them, like Sophia's report about Mr. Mzee. Include:
Who the person is and what they do.
Three things they do in their daily work.
One thing you learned from them.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about an interview report.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2: My Daily Life
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a paragraph called “My Daily Life”. Use at least three adverbs (daily, usually, really, clearly) and join some ideas with and, but, or because. Finish with one sentence about how you keep a good balance.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about my daily life.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

activities.title: Activities 1: True or False
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
“Daily” means something that happens once a week.
To “maintain” something means to keep it in good condition.
A “citizen” is a person who belongs to a country.
To “cancel” something means to make it happen.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Choose the Verb Pattern
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose the correct word in brackets.
She agrees ______ (to send / sending) the mail.
They enjoy ______ (to play / playing) football after school.
He wants ______ (to learn / learning) a new language.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Fill in the Adverb
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Fill each blank with an adverb: daily, usually, really, clearly.
I ______ check the mail every morning.
She spoke ______ so everyone could hear her.
I ______ enjoy our reading time in class.
We ______ eat lunch at noon.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Join the Sentences
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Join each pair with and, but, or because.
I write the mail. I maintain the records.
I wanted to play outside. It started to rain.
She runs fast. She has a lot of speed.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Daily Life & Communication learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1: An Interview Report and a recording of Speaking 1: Describe Your Daily Routine. Improve both pieces using the Grade 4 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'mail' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who belongs to a country and has rights there. | Letters and packages that are sent or delivered. | To have the same opinion, or to say yes to something. | To keep doing something without stopping.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Letters and packages that are sent or delivered.
quizzes.explanation: I received a letter in the mail today.

quizzes.question: What does 'language' mean?
quizzes.options: A way people talk and write to share ideas and feelings. | To get something you want, or to become better at something. | To have the same opinion, or to say yes to something. | To keep doing something without stopping.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A way people talk and write to share ideas and feelings.
quizzes.explanation: English is a language that many people speak around the world.

quizzes.question: What does 'citizen' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who belongs to a country and has rights there. | The hard work you put into doing something. | To get something you want, or to become better at something. | To keep doing something without stopping.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who belongs to a country and has rights there.
quizzes.explanation: Every citizen has rights and rules to follow.

quizzes.question: What does 'agree' mean?
quizzes.options: The hard work you put into doing something. | To get something you want, or to become better at something. | To have the same opinion, or to say yes to something. | To learn something very well.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To have the same opinion, or to say yes to something.
quizzes.explanation: I agree with you that reading is fun.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Day at the Posthouse': Every day he sorts the _____, helps people send letters, and reads letters aloud for those who cannot read well.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Every day he sorts the mail, helps people send letters, and reads letters aloud for those who cannot read well.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Fair Effort, Daily Gains': She wanted to improve her _____ and master new words, and a dictionary would help her learn faster.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: She wanted to improve her language and master new words, and a dictionary would help her learn faster.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Fair Effort, Daily Gains': Mzee, the kind _____ who worked at the posthouse.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Mzee, the kind citizen who worked at the posthouse.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Fair Effort, Daily Gains': “I will try my best and _____ learning.” She thought about who to write about.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “I will try my best and continue learning.” She thought about who to write about.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Day at the Posthouse': His work is not fast, but he does it with _____ and care.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: His work is not fast, but he does it with effort and care.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Fair Effort, Daily Gains': “I had to _____ English and Kiswahili.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “I had to master English and Kiswahili.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the interview with me, and let me play Sophia?”
“Can you play a listening game about daily routines?”
“Can you explain any words in the reading that I did not understand?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise describing my daily routine?”
“Can you be the person I interview, and answer my questions?”
“Can you check that I use adverbs in the right place?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my interview report and tell me what to fix?”
“Did I use conjunctions correctly in my paragraph?”
“Can you help me make my report clear and fair?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you give me another example sentence for this word: ______?”
“What is the difference between ‘agree’ and ‘continue’?”
“Can you quiz me on Group 1 words?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me use ‘master’ as a verb in a sentence?”
“Can you give me more practice with ‘gain’ and ‘maintain’?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on the adjectives in Group 3?”
“What is the opposite of ‘fair’ and ‘usual’?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Where do I put the adverb ‘really’ in a sentence?”
“Can you give me more adverbs I can use, like ‘really’ and ‘clearly’?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘she likes’ but ‘I like’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise verb patterns?”

answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Letters and packages that are sent or delivered. I received a letter in the mail today.
A way people talk and write to share ideas and feelings. English is a language that many people speak around the world.
A person who belongs to a country and has rights there. Every citizen has rights and rules to follow.
To have the same opinion, or to say yes to something. I agree with you that reading is fun.
To keep doing something without stopping. Please continue reading your book after lunch.
To get something you want, or to become better at something. I want to gain new skills by practising every day.
The hard work you put into doing something. It took a lot of effort to finish my homework on time.
To learn something very well. She mastered the dance after many tries.
To keep something in good condition, or to make it stay the same. I help my father maintain the garden by watering the plants.
How fast something moves or happens. The car moved at a high speed.

selfAssessment.statement: I can talk and write about my daily routine.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use verb patterns like “agree to send”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can add adverbs to say how and how often.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use “have got” and “has got”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can ask clear Wh- and interview questions.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can give instructions and join ideas with conjunctions.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
